name: Andalusi Arabic
notes: Andalusi Arabic (8th–17th c. CE). Western (Maghrebi-type) Arabic dialect bundle of al-Andalus; the best-documented medieval vernacular Arabic, attested in zaǧal (earliest 913 CE), ḫaraǧāt, Pedro de Alcalá (1505), the anonymous Vocabulista and the proverb collections. DEFINING FEATURES (Corriente et al. 2015; Vicente 2020): (1) IMĀLA — raising of etymological /aː/ to [eː] and, in strong (Granadan) imāla, [iː]: qitāl→qitēl/qitīl 'battle', wālid→wīld 'father'; the negator lā appears as lás/lís (corriente2013). (2) STRESS-based prosody replacing OA quantitative rhythm: no phonemic vowel length; stress is contrastive and marked in sources by anomalous matres lectionis — /muqáṣṣ/ 'scissors', /usqúf/ 'bishop', /qunfúd/ 'hedgehog', /alandalús/, /azzahár/ 'orange blossom' (Romance imposition; vicente2020 §3.1.1; corriente2015:75–78). (3) Three OA interdentals /θ ð ðˤ/ retained (Bedouin-type), occlusive realisation stigmatised (corriente2015:29); /q/ voiceless [q] (sedentary-type), not [g] (vicente2020). (4) Spirantisation of postvocalic /d/→[ð] under Romance contact: ǧaðwal 'creek', ḥafīð 'nephew', al-ḥaðð 'Sunday', sīði 'my lord' (corriente2015 via vicente2020); [β] allophone of /b/. (5) Partial loss of contrastive velarisation: /s/~/sˤ/ interchange (sūr~ṣūr). (6) Marginal Romance-origin phonemes /p/ /g/ /č/ from loans: čípp 'trap', čiqála 'cicada' (corriente1978). (7) Heavy Romance adstrate — hybrid suffixes (-ūn augmentative, -áyr agentive), Romance-model gender/agreement, min/li calques (naḫruǧ li wildī 'I look like my father'). ~2000+ Andalusi Arabic loanwords survive in Ibero-Romance (alcalde, algodón, azúcar, alfombra...). NOTE: the o2i engine inherits the arb inventory and does NOT yet realise imāla or destressing (it emits OA-style long vowels); the gold is engine-pinned and these two traits are documented here as spec/engine leads, not bent into the transcriptions.
